title: Roast spatchcock chicken
subtitle: Butterflied whole chicken with lemon and herbs
excerpt: A whole chicken butterflied flat, seasoned with lemon, garlic, and herbs, and roasted at high heat until the skin is golden and crisp and the meat is cooked through in under an hour. Faster and crispier than a traditional whole roast.
sourceNotes: Spatchcocking, which involves removing the backbone of a bird to flatten it for even cooking, is documented in British cookery from at least the eighteenth century. Hannah Glasse mentions the technique in The Art of Cookery (1747). The method reduces cooking time significantly and produces very crisp skin.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Spatchcock and marinate up to 24 hours ahead in the fridge. Bring to room temperature before roasting.

content.text: Spatchcocking solves the main problem with whole roast chicken: the breast cooks faster than the thigh and is dry before the legs are done. By flattening the bird, all parts are at roughly the same distance from the heat and cook in 40 to 45 minutes rather than 90. The skin becomes uniformly crisp. It is worth learning the technique.

A 1.5 kg free-range chicken is the right size for four people with a generous side dish. The backbone comes out with kitchen shears in about 30 seconds; the cartilage at the breastbone cracks flat under firm palm pressure. The whole operation takes under 5 minutes.

Place {{whole-chicken}} chicken breast-side down on a board. Using kitchen shears, cut along both sides of the backbone and remove it entirely. Flip the bird over and press firmly on the breastbone with the heel of your hand until it cracks flat. The chicken should now lie in one flat piece.

Mix {{olive-oil}} of olive oil with {{garlic}} of crushed garlic, the lemon zest, chopped thyme, chopped rosemary, and smoked paprika if using. Season very generously with salt. Rub this mixture all over the chicken, including under the skin where possible. If time allows, refrigerate for at least 30 minutes or up to 24 hours.

Preheat the oven to 220°C fan. Place the chicken skin-side up in a roasting tin. Tuck the lemon halves around it. Roast for 40 to 45 minutes until the skin is deeply golden and the juices from the thickest part of the thigh run clear. The internal temperature at the thigh should be 75°C or above. Rest on a board for 10 minutes, loosely covered, before cutting into portions.

items.cause: Chicken was cold from the fridge, or surface was wet
items.fix: Bring the chicken to room temperature before roasting and pat dry with kitchen paper. A very cold bird will steam before the skin crisps.

items.symptom: Juices are still pink at 45 minutes
items.cause: Bird is larger than 1.5 kg, or oven runs cool
items.fix: Continue roasting and check every 5 minutes. A thermometer reading of 75°C at the thigh is the reliable indicator.

items.cause: Cooked for too long or oven temperature too low, causing extended cooking time
items.fix: Spatchcocked chicken cooks faster precisely because all parts are equidistant. 220°C fan for 40 to 45 minutes is correct for 1.5 kg. Using a thermometer prevents overcooking.

items.cause: Kitchen shears are not sharp enough
items.fix: Sharp kitchen shears or poultry shears cut through bone cleanly. A heavy knife and a steady board also work. Cut firmly from one end to the other along each side.

content.text: Spatchcocking is one of the older techniques in British cookery, mentioned in Hannah Glasse's The Art of Cookery in 1747 and common in earlier centuries when birds were cooked over open fires where the flat presentation was a practical advantage. The word spatchcock is of uncertain etymology — possibly a corruption of dispatch the cock, referring to the rapid preparation and cooking of a bird. In twentieth-century domestic cooking the technique fell out of regular use as the closed oven became standard, but it has returned to favour as cooks rediscovered its speed and the quality of the resulting skin.
